# UI Improvement Suggestions
<!-- Generated by /ui-review on 2026-05-07 -->
<!-- Reference: frontend_design.md (design intent), backend_info.md (API constraints) -->

## How to use this file
An agent should read this file, implement the improvements, then delete each item as it is resolved.
Where a suggestion deviates from `frontend_design.md`, the implementing agent should also update that file to reflect the new decision.
Re-run `/ui-review` after implementing to verify changes and regenerate fresh suggestions.

---

## P1 — Broken or Missing
These block usability or violate the API contract. Fix before anything else.

### Score column header label uses `%` instead of `score` — and the values are not percentages
- **Stage:** Stage 6
- **Spec section:** §8 ("Score column headers" — "Use 'score' (not '%') for consistency…"), §9 (score range table).
- **Observed:** The per-column score header in the results table renders as e.g. `SPECIFIC ENERGY (WH/KG) %` (small `%` glyph appended). The cells underneath show 0–1 floats like `0.950`, **not** percentages. The Sort-by dropdown in the same toolbar correctly says `Specific Energy (Wh/kg) score`, so the table header is internally inconsistent with the dropdown.
- **Problem:** This is both a spec deviation and a correctness issue. `%` on a column whose values are scalars in [0, 1] is misleading to engineers — they will reasonably read `0.950 %` as "less than one percent." The spec explicitly mandates `score` for exactly this reason ("`<option>` cannot render styled HTML, so use the same word everywhere"). The current code unilaterally swapped to `%` only in the table header, breaking that contract.
- **Suggestion:** In `frontend/src/components/ResultsTable.jsx:131-142`, replace the `<span>%</span>` suffix in `colHeader()` with the literal text ` score` (matching `ResultsView.jsx:73` and `FilterPanel.jsx:6-10`). Optionally drop the inline span styling — a plain trailing `score` keeps the header readable under the existing `text-transform: uppercase` table-header style (`SPECIFIC ENERGY (WH/KG) SCORE`).

---

## P2 — Design Issues
Meaningful UX/UI improvements — whether they fix spec deviations or go beyond the spec.

### Spec parameter dropdown corrupts SI unit casing (`Wh/kg` → `Wh/Kg`)
- **Stage:** Stage 4
- **Spec section:** §8 ("Never title-case content inside parentheses (SI unit notation like 'W/kg' must not become 'W/Kg')").
- **Observed:** The Parameter `<select>` in each spec row shows option labels like `Specific Energy (Wh/Kg)` and `Volumetric Energy Density (Wh/L)`. The actual API column name is `Specific Energy (Wh/kg)` (lowercase `kg`). The `value` attribute is correct, but the visible text is corrupted. The same dropdown also produces `Wh/Kg` even though the value-input adornment to its right correctly displays `Wh/kg`, so the row ends up self-contradictory.
- **Why it could be better:** `SpecRow.jsx:11` defines `fmt(s)` as `s.replace(/_/g, ' ').replace(/\b\w/g, c => c.toUpperCase())`. The `\b\w` pattern matches the first letter of every word — including the `k` in `kg` after the `/` boundary. The score-column logic in `ResultsTable.jsx:scoreColLabel` already implements the correct rule (split on first `(`, only title-case the prefix, re-append the parenthesized portion verbatim).
- **Suggestion:** Replace `fmt()` in `SpecRow.jsx` with the `scoreColLabel`-style helper (extract it into `frontend/src/utils/format.js` and reuse). Apply it to the parameter dropdown options. Also audit any other call sites of `fmt()` that might receive strings containing parentheses; the `ResultsView.jsx:fmt` (used for the breadcrumb) is fine because solution/system names don't contain parens.

### Score Breakdown panel corrupts SI unit casing via CSS `text-transform: capitalize`
- **Stage:** Stage 9
- **Spec section:** §8 ("parameter name (140px, capitalize)" — but the spec also forbids capitalizing inside parens).
- **Observed:** Expanding a result row shows the breakdown row with parameter label `Specific Energy (Wh/Kg)` — capitalized `K`. Confirmed via `getComputedStyle`: `.breakdown-param` has `text-transform: capitalize`. The DOM text is correct (`Specific Energy (Wh/kg)`); the corruption is purely visual via CSS.
- **Why it could be better:** CSS `capitalize` uppercases the first letter of every space-separated word, which catches the letter immediately after `(`. This silently breaks SI unit conventions for engineers who expect `Wh/kg`, `m/s²`, `µm`, etc.
- **Suggestion:** Drop `text-transform: capitalize` from `.breakdown-param`. Either (a) leave the column name as-is (engineering names are already canonical from the DB), or (b) pre-format via JS using the same `scoreColLabel`-style helper proposed above. Option (a) is simpler and preserves correctness. The spec line that calls for `capitalize` should be updated to clarify "no transform — display the column name as stored."

### Score bar overlaid label has poor contrast on light tier colors
- **Stage:** Stage 6
- **Spec section:** §9 ("score-excellent #22c55e", "score-good #84cc16", "score-fair #f59e0b").
- **Observed:** The overall_score bar renders the numeric label in `#fff` 700 weight on the tier-color fill. Measured contrast: white on `#22c55e` (excellent green) ≈ 1.7:1; on `#84cc16` (good lime) ≈ 1.5:1; on `#f59e0b` (fair amber) ≈ 1.9:1. All fall below WCAG AA's 4.5:1 minimum (and below the 3:1 large-text threshold). On the screenshots, the digit `5` in `0.950` and `0.853` is hard to read at normal monitor brightness.
- **Why it could be better:** The overall score is the most prominent piece of information in the table. If it's hard to read at a glance, the whole point of the score-bar treatment is undermined. The score chip already has good contrast (full tier color on dark page) — the bar should match.
- **Suggestion:** In `frontend/src/components/ui/ScoreBar.jsx`, render the label in a tier-aware color: dark text (`#0a1929` or `#000`) for light tiers (excellent/good/fair) and white only for the poor tier (`#ef4444`). A tiny helper next to `scoreColor()` can return the foreground:
  ```js
  export function scoreLabelColor(val) {
    if (val == null || val < 0.4) return '#fff';
    return '#0a1929';
  }
  ```
  Alternatively, keep the white text but add `text-shadow: 0 1px 2px rgba(0,0,0,0.5)` for legibility — slightly less crisp but a 1-line fix.

### Solution/system dropdowns mechanically capitalize, mangling acronyms
- **Stage:** Stage 1
- **Spec section:** §4 (no explicit guidance).
- **Observed:** The Solution Type dropdown shows `Gnc` (should read as `GNC`, the standard aerospace acronym for "Guidance, Navigation, and Control"). The System dropdown shows `Management And Distribution` (English style would be `Management and Distribution` — lowercase "and"). These come from `fmt()` in `ScopeSection.jsx:3-5`, which uppercases every word's first letter.
- **Why it could be better:** Aerospace engineers will read `Gnc` as a typo. While the data is sourced from PostgreSQL schema/table names (Rule 3), the *transformation* that produces this output is in the frontend, so the frontend can fix it.
- **Suggestion:** Add a small display-name override map keyed by raw schema/table name (similar to `KwargField.FRIENDLY_NAMES`). Examples: `gnc` → `GNC`, `management_and_distribution` → `Management and Distribution`, `trl` → `TRL`. Long-term, the cleaner fix is to expose display names from the API (e.g., a new `display_name` field on the schema/table list endpoints), but the override map is the smallest immediately-shippable change. Note this in `frontend_design.md` §4 if adopted.

### Apply button is visually indistinguishable from the adjacent Filters button
- **Stage:** Stage 7
- **Spec section:** §8 ("Apply uses `.btn-secondary`… The Search button keeps the filled blue primary style. This visual separation is required to communicate the two-speed workflow.")
- **Observed:** The toolbar shows `[Filters] [⟳ Apply]` adjacent to each other, both styled `.btn-secondary` (transparent + outlined). They look like a button pair where one is a toggle and the other is an action, but at a glance they read as the same control type.
- **Why it could be better:** The two-speed workflow (fast Apply vs slow Search) is a key UX concept the spec emphasises. Filters is a *toggle*; Apply is the actual *fast reslice trigger*. Conflating them visually weakens the very thing the spec calls out as critical.
- **Suggestion:** Push Filters one step toward "toggle" affordance (e.g., a darker pill background when open, lighter outline when closed; treat it as a state-toggle visual). Keep Apply as the only "action" button in the toolbar. Add a subtle vertical divider (`border-left: 1px solid var(--border-subtle)` with `padding-left: 12px`) between Filters and Apply to mark them as different concepts. Reflect the change in `frontend_design.md` §8.

### Sidebar lacks a visible "Step 1 / Scope" section title
- **Stage:** Stage 1
- **Spec section:** §3 (sidebar shell shows `Scope` as a section), §4 (Step 1 — Scope Section).
- **Observed:** The sidebar renders `SOLUTION TYPE` and `COMPONENT SYSTEM` as small uppercase labels, but there is no parent section heading the way `Match Specifications` clearly heads Step 2. The spec's shell ASCII diagram shows `Scope` as a section in the sidebar.
- **Why it could be better:** With `Match Specifications` clearly labelled as a section, and the scope dropdowns silent, the visual hierarchy implies "specs are a section, scope is just two stray inputs." Adding a header makes the two-step flow explicit (which is what the spec was going for).
- **Suggestion:** In `ScopeSection.jsx`, add `<div className="section-title">Search Scope</div>` (or `Step 1 — Scope`) above the two `scope-row` divs, mirroring `SpecBuilder.jsx:17`. If you don't want the extra vertical space pre-selection, hide it under `isCompact` — but at minimum make Step 1's section label exist for parity with Step 2.

### Empty filter panel shows only "+ Add Filter" with no first row or hint
- **Stage:** Stage 8
- **Spec section:** §8 (filter panel layout).
- **Observed:** Clicking `Filters` for the first time reveals the panel with zero filter rows and just the `+ Add Filter` dashed button. There is no copy explaining what filters do, no first row pre-populated.
- **Why it could be better:** Two extra clicks to add the first filter (open + add). For an engineering tool where filtering scored results is a common path, the expected flow is "open filters → start filtering."
- **Suggestion:** Either (a) auto-add one empty filter row on first open if `filters.length === 0`, or (b) replace the empty state with a one-line hint above the button: `"Filter scored results by score thresholds or numeric ranges. Add a filter below."` Option (a) is more direct.

### Pagination only shows "of N pages" — no "showing X–Y of Z components"
- **Stage:** Stage 7
- **Spec section:** §8 (toolbar copy describes `← 1 of 5 →`).
- **Observed:** Toolbar pagination reads `[1] of 2`. The user has to mentally calculate which rows they're seeing (e.g., page 1 with 10/page = rows 1–10 of 13).
- **Why it could be better:** Engineers comparing components want to know how many of the total they're currently inspecting. "Showing 1–10 of 13" is industry-standard and reads at a glance.
- **Suggestion:** Add a small caption next to the page input or under the toolbar: `Showing {start}–{end} of {totalResults}`, where `start = (page-1)*perPage + 1` and `end = Math.min(page*perPage, totalResults)`. This deviates from the spec's exact toolbar layout — note the deviation in `frontend_design.md` §8.

### Scope section's compact state isn't compact enough — large gap before Match Specifications
- **Stage:** Stage 3
- **Spec section:** §4 ("reduce section vertical padding from 20px to 12px").
- **Observed:** After both Solution and System are selected, the scope section's compact state engages but the visible padding reduction is subtle. Combined with the "Match Specifications" section title, hint paragraph, and empty-state card, the spec builder consumes a lot of vertical real estate before the user can even see their first spec row.
- **Why it could be better:** On a 1280-pixel-tall display the user can lose Add Specification below the fold once they have ~3 specs and have opened Advanced. Tightening the chrome around the scope section gives more room to specs (the actual content the user is editing).
- **Suggestion:** Reduce vertical spacing in the compact `.sidebar-section.compact` rule, drop the section-title margins inside Match Specifications, and consider hiding the "Add at least one specification…" empty hint once the user clicks Add Specification (the empty hint is informational; once a spec exists it's noise).

### Filter row crowds 5 controls + remove on one line at default sidebar width
- **Stage:** Stage 8
- **Spec section:** §8 ("[ Column ▼ ]   ≥  [ 0.00 ]   ≤  [ 1.00 ]   [✕ Remove]").
- **Observed:** The filter row puts column select, ≥ symbol, min input, ≤ symbol, max input, and remove button on one row. With the sidebar at default 400px, the remaining ~880px still feels cramped — column select gets ~340px and inputs get only ~200px each. Multi-filter use will compress further.
- **Why it could be better:** Numeric inputs near the right edge of the toolbar can clip; the /clerelationship between the column select and its bounds is harder to scan when everything is mashed together.
- **Suggestion:** Either (a) wrap on narrow widths (allow `flex-wrap: wrap` on `.filter-row`), or (b) put the column select on its own line and min/max bounds on a sub-row beneath. The two-row treatment makes the relationship between column and its bounds explicit at any width.

### Apply button doesn't distinguish "no changes pending" from "changes pending"
- **Stage:** Stage 7
- **Spec section:** §8 (Apply button behavior).
- **Observed:** The Apply button is always clickable (unless mid-request). Clicking it when nothing has changed still issues a `POST /search/{session_id}` with the current state. There's no signal that pressing Apply right now would be a no-op.
- **Why it could be better:** For a fast-but-not-free reslice, an "is dirty" indicator helps users understand the model. Conversely, after changing sort or filters and pressing Apply, the button could go back to a neutral state.
- **Suggestion:** Track a `dirty` flag in state — set true on sort/filter/per-page change, false after a successful Apply or Search. Render Apply as a low-emphasis button when `!dirty`. Note: sort and per-page already auto-apply, so the only "dirty source" today is filter edits — making this a small but meaningful improvement scoped to filters.

### No indicator that the sidebar specs have diverged from the searched specs
- **Stage:** Stage 6
- **Spec section:** §8 ("There is no 'Modify Search' button — it is redundant on a desktop layout where the sidebar is always visible.")
- **Observed:** The spec deliberately omits a Modify Search button — the sidebar is always visible, so the user can edit specs in place. When they do, `state.specs` updates immediately but `state.searchedSpecs` stays frozen at the last search. The results header remains correctly tied to `searchedSpecs`, but there is no visual cue that the sidebar and the results are now describing different searches.
- **Why it could be better:** Engineers iterating on values may forget which version they last submitted. A passive indicator preserves the spec's "no extra button" preference while improving clarity.
- **Suggestion:** Compare `state.specs` to `state.searchedSpecs`; when they differ, render a small badge near the Search button or above the results header: `"Sidebar has unsearched changes — press Search to refresh."` This is additive to the spec, not a deviation.

---

## P3 — Polish
Minor refinements that improve quality without changing core functionality.

### Component System dropdown disabled state is visually too similar to enabled
- **Stage:** Stage 1
- **Observed:** Before a solution is selected, the Component System select is `disabled` in DOM but visually nearly identical to the enabled Solution Type select above it (same border, same chevron, similar text color).
- **Suggestion:** Reduce `opacity` to `0.5` (or apply `:disabled` selector with a darker background and a `not-allowed` cursor) on the disabled state in the global select styles.

### Score chip text contrast on dark navy is borderline for the green tiers
- **Stage:** Stage 6
- **Observed:** Score chip uses tier-color text (e.g. `#22c55e`) on `tier-color × 0.157` background. On the page's `#060d1a` base, the effective bg is a slightly green-tinted dark navy. Green-on-dark works, but the bright lime (`#84cc16`) tier text is desaturated.
- **Suggestion:** Slightly bump chip text brightness (e.g. mix tier color with white at 10–15%) for better legibility, or accept the trade-off and add a small drop-shadow to the chip text.

### Reset (✕) icons next to label rows are tiny (12px) and discoverable only on hover
- **Stage:** Stage 2/3
- **Observed:** The small `✕` next to "Solution Type" and "Component System" labels resets the choice. At 12px, they're easy to miss; the only signal that they're interactive is the hover cursor and a "Reset solution" tooltip.
- **Suggestion:** Bump to 14px and add a subtle `hover` background (e.g. `rgba(255,255,255,0.06)`) to make them feel like buttons. Already wrapped in `btn-icon` — likely just a sizing tweak.

### Empty space between Add Specification and Advanced Scoring sections
- **Stage:** Stage 4
- **Observed:** The sidebar uses `<div style={{ flex: 1 }} />` to push the Search button to the bottom, leaving large dead space when the user has 1–2 specs. Visually, the Search button feels orphaned from the Match Specifications section above.
- **Suggestion:** Either (a) place Advanced Scoring directly under specs without the spacer (then sticky-bottom only the Search button), or (b) add a faint horizontal rule above the Search button so the gap reads as "below the fold of editable content."

### Score bar fill at 100% has no end-cap differentiation from "very high but not perfect"
- **Stage:** Stage 6
- **Observed:** A perfect 1.000 score and a 0.999 score look essentially identical in the bar (full vs ~99% width). Both render the same color tier.
- **Suggestion:** Optional: add a subtle ★ glyph or gold edge for ≥ 0.99 — engineers love a clear "perfect match" signal.

### Boolean kwargs default text says `Default: true` (lowercase) instead of `Default: True`
- **Stage:** Stage 4
- **Observed:** Under the Advanced "Use dataset extrema" toggle, the muted hint reads `Default: true`. Since the user-visible toggle pills are `True` / `False` (capitalised), the lowercase default copy is mildly inconsistent.
- **Suggestion:** In `KwargField.jsx`, capitalise booleans for display: `kwarg.default === true ? 'True' : kwarg.default === false ? 'False' : kwarg.default.toString()`.

### Sort dropdown groups score columns first; data columns appear lowercase-with-spaces-from-DB
- **Stage:** Stage 7
- **Observed:** Score Columns optgroup shows friendly `Specific Energy (Wh/kg) score`. Data Columns optgroup uses raw column names — they happen to be space-separated already, so they look fine. If a future schema introduces an underscored column name (e.g. `cells_used`), it would show raw.
- **Suggestion:** Apply a single, parens-aware formatter to *all* dropdown labels (sort, filter, parameter) so future data is robust to formatting drift.

### `Type: number` subtitle below parameter dropdown is helpful but undersized
- **Stage:** Stage 4
- **Observed:** Helpful affordance — tells users what kind of value the dtype expects. But it's small and muted; engineers may not register it.
- **Suggestion:** Slightly bump the dtype tag's font-weight or use a subtle background pill, e.g. `.spec-dtype-tag { background: rgba(255,255,255,0.05); padding: 1px 6px; border-radius: 3px; }`.

### Breakdown row title "SCORE BREAKDOWN" is very small/dim
- **Stage:** Stage 9
- **Observed:** The expanded row's "SCORE BREAKDOWN" header is 11px uppercase muted text. It's hard to spot when scanning a long results table; if multi-row expansion is added later, anchored section heads matter even more.
- **Suggestion:** Bump to 12px and slightly brighter color, or add a thin top-border on `.breakdown-panel` so the section is visually anchored.

### Solution dropdown is missing a "no solutions available" empty-list state
- **Stage:** Stage 1
- **Observed:** If `GET /options` returns an empty list (e.g., empty database), the dropdown silently shows only the placeholder. No message, no error.
- **Suggestion:** When `solutions.length === 0` and `loadingSolutions === false`, surface an inline message: `No solutions available — check the data pipeline.` Same treatment for empty systems.

---

## Stages With No Issues
- **Stage 0** — Header, palette, typography all read correctly. Iconic blue satellite + wordmark looks professional.
- **Stage 5** — Search button states are clear: "Add a specification to search" → "Fill in all specification values" → "Search Components →". Disabled treatment is clear.
- **Stage 10 (holistic)** — Information architecture is broadly correct; the two-speed model is *implementable* (the pieces are in place — just needs the visual reinforcement called out in P2 above). Color tier system is applied consistently across bars, chips, breakdown bars. Sticky overall_score column on horizontal scroll is correctly implemented (verified at scrollLeft=800 the score column stays at viewport-left). No console errors or warnings observed during the walkthrough.
